content_preview.title: OpenAI Open-Sources Symphony, an Apache-Licensed Spec That Turns Linear Into a Control Plane for Codex Agents
content_preview.summary: OpenAI published Symphony on April 28 as an open-source specification for orchestrating Codex coding agents directly off a Linear board, with internal teams reporting a 500% jump in landed pull requests.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

OpenAI on April 28, 2026 published [Symphony](https://github.com/openai/symphony), an open-source specification and reference implementation that turns an issue tracker like Linear into a control plane for autonomous coding agents. According to [InfoWorld](https://www.infoworld.com/article/4164173/openais-symphony-spec-pushes-coding-agents-from-prompts-to-orchestration.html), Symphony is "an open-source specification for turning issue trackers such as Linear into control planes for ...

recommendations: Before merging, the human reviewer should write a corrections file at src/content/corrections/<YYYY-MM>/<article-slug>.json documenting the warning(s).
Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Strong 776-word News piece on OpenAI's Symphony release (Apr 28, 2026). Structure (Overview / What We Know / How the Workflow Hangs Together / What We Don't Know) is solid. The article goes deep into spec mechanics (state machine, polling defaults, GraphQL integration, lifecycle) without becoming a regurgitation of the README. The 'What We Don't Know' section properly bounds the 500% claim as OpenAI's own internal metric.
editor_notes.source_verification:
All 5 source snapshots read; every claim verified verbatim against the underlying material.

• source-0.html.gz (GitHub repo openai/symphony): Confirms repo description 'Symphony turns project work into isolated, autonomous implementation runs, allowing teams to manage work instead of supervising coding agents' (verbatim); 'low-key engineering preview for testing in trusted environments' (verbatim); Apache-2.0 license; 21.5k stars (article says 'more than 21,000'); 1.9k forks (article says 'over 1,900'); Elixir as primary language (95.5%).

• source-1.html.gz (SPEC.md): Confirms 'long-running automation service that continuously reads work from an issue tracker (Linear in this specification version), creates an isolated workspace for each issue, and runs a coding agent session for that issue inside the workspace' (verbatim); poll interval default 30000 ms (= 30 seconds); all five phases (PreparingWorkspace, BuildingPrompt, LaunchingAgentProcess, StreamingTurn, Finishing); all five terminal states (Succeeded, Failed, TimedOut, Stalled, CanceledByReconciliation); stall_timeout_ms default 300000 (= 5 minutes); exponential backoff retry; LINEAR_API_KEY env var; api.linear.app/graphql endpoint; bash -lc launch model; WORKFLOW.md YAML front matter + prompt body structure.

• source-2.html.gz (elixir/README.md): Confirms App Server mode launch; linear_graphql tool exposed; Phoenix stack with LiveView dashboard, JSON API at /api/v1/*, Bandit HTTP server; max_turns default 20.

• source-3.html.gz (InfoWorld): Confirms verbatim 'an open-source specification for turning issue trackers such as Linear into control planes for Codex coding agents'; 500% landed-PRs increase 'in the first three weeks' on 'some internal teams'; verbatim list 'monitor issue states, restart agents that crash or stall, manage per-issue workspaces, watch CI, rebase changes, resolve conflicts, and shepherd pull requests toward review'.

• source-4.html.gz (Gizmodo): Confirms verbatim 'every open task gets an agent, agents run continuously, and humans review the results'.

Every inline link points to a source containing the specific claim. No misattribution. No fabricated quantitative claims.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All factual claims verified. The article correctly handles the 500% figure as OpenAI's internal claim (in 'What We Don't Know' section) rather than treating it as an independent benchmark. Spec details (defaults, state machine, GraphQL plumbing) match SPEC.md exactly.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral, technical, appropriately bounded. The 'How the Workflow Hangs Together' section is interpretation but stays close to spec language and credits Gizmodo for the framing. No editorialising on whether the approach is good or bad.
editor_notes.originality: Searched archive for 'Symphony', 'OpenAI Symphony', 'Codex orchestrator' — no prior coverage. Topic is genuinely new.
recommendations: Future housekeeping: infoworld.com is a reputable enterprise-tech publication and a candidate for the allowlist. github.com remains too broad to allowlist globally.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS. High-quality, technically detailed coverage with every claim sourced and verified verbatim. Verdict driven by the github.com (3 entries — primary-source repo links) and infoworld.com (1 entry) allowlist warnings. Article is publishable as-is.
